data.title: Core & Main's Bright Outlook Doesn't Justify Any Additional Optimism
data.description:
I always appreciate companies that are showing attractive growth. But as a value investor, the price for that growth has to be appropriate. In some instances, it is. In others, it's not even close. Back in December when I last looked at Core & Main, Inc. (CNM), I found myself impressed with the overall financial performance of the business. However, I made the case that, for all the growth that the company was exhibiting, upside potential was likely limited from that point on. This led me to take a more neutral stance on the business, ultimately calling it a Hold. Since that time, the stock has performed along the lines of what I would have anticipated. While the S&P 500 (SP500) is down 3.8%, its stock is down 3.4%. This is even in spite of a 4.5% jump in share price that we saw on March 24th after management reported financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year.

Those figures were actually mixed. Revenue, for instance, fell short of analysts’ expectations. But at the same time, earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share both came in above what they were anticipating. What's more, management is forecasting some growth this year, specifically on the top line and when it comes to EBITDA. But at the end of the day, the stock just does not look cheap enough on an absolute basis to justify all that bullish outlook. Instead, it makes for a much better Hold candidate than it does a Buy right now.

A Mixed Bag

The final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was an interesting time for shareholders of Core & Main. Take revenue, for starters. It actually fell year over year, dropping from $1.70 billion to $1.58 billion. At first glance, this is a bit surprising. I say this because, for the year as a whole, revenue grew 2.8%, from $7.44 billion to $7.65 billion. The decline in sales, according to management, was actually because of an extra selling week that the company had in 2024. Actual average daily net sales for the company grew 0.9%. This was attributable to higher volumes and higher average selling prices associated with its fire protection products. Even though actual revenue for that category remained flat year over year, if we strip out the extra week that the company had in 2024, revenue would have risen.

The greatest growth rate for the company for the quarter ended up being its meter products category, though. Revenue jumped 4% year over year, climbing from $174 million to $181 million. And for the year as a whole, the 3.5% revenue expansion that was seen there was only surpassed by the storm drainage products category that saw a 4.1% jump in revenue for the year but an 11.5% drop in the final quarter. The smart meter category fascinates me because management has already achieved a 14% annualized growth rate in this space over the last five years. This happens to be a subsegment of the broader meters category. But management expects that it will become more important as time goes on. They expect double-digit revenue growth for smart meters this year, as an example. And the company is doing this not just as a regular supplier, but instead as what it now describes as a ‘preferred prime contractor’ for large and complex projects. This could set the company up to become increasingly the go-to source for major project planners when it comes to this technology.

As revenue for the business improved, profitability followed suit. But unlike revenue, which came in $8 million below what analysts were hoping, profitability exceeded expectations. Earnings per share rose from $0.33 to $0.37. That ended up being $0.03 above what analysts were hoping to see. And it meant a rise in net profits from $64 million to $70 million. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share inched higher from $0.51 to $0.52. While that doesn't seem impressive, it actually ended up being $0.04 greater than what analysts were hoping to see. But because of changes in the company share count and rounding, adjusted net profits remained flat at $102 million.

According to management, the company benefited from a $15 million drop in selling, general, and administrative costs. But again, this was because of a difference in the number of weeks that the company had for each quarter. But on top of this, the firm has benefited from cost reduction initiatives that more than compensated for inflationary pressures and investments that the company made in both personnel and technology. As a percentage of revenue, however, these expenses did increase. But the good news is that the gross profit margin for the company ended up rising from 26.6% to 27.1%. That pop was because of certain gross margin initiatives, as well as what management described as disciplined purchasing and pricing management.

Other profitability metrics ended up being mixed. Operating cash flow, for instance, rose from $235 million to $268 million. But if we strip out changes in working capital, we get it flatlining at $122 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted from $179 million to $167 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 compared to 2024. Despite the rough quarter that was admittedly caused by the aforementioned calendar difference, 2025 in its entirety ended up being a positive time for the company. Without exception, every metric in that chart improved year over year. What's more, it's looking as though we should expect further improvement in 2026. Management currently anticipates revenue of between $7.80 billion and $7.90 billion. At the midpoint, that would be 2.7% above what the company saw last year.

Management does expect additional pain this year when it comes to the residential space. That's not surprising, as I have detailed in other articles. But the good news is that only 18% of the company's revenue comes from that market. The non-residential space, meanwhile, is much larger at about 38% of sales. And even though there is strength in some areas like data centers and infrastructure, the office and retail markets will offset this, leading to flat revenue for the year. The real surprise is the municipal market that is responsible for 44% of revenue. Non-discretionary spending and reliable funding should lead to a low single-digit growth rate for the company.

Management hopes that their efforts to continue focusing on certain growth opportunities, like smart meters that saw a 14% jump in revenue year over year, the water treatment category that saw growth of about 25%, and other similar opportunities, will help to push the company's market share up throughout the year. They are hoping to capture between 2% and 4% market share gain for 2026. Considering that the firm boasts a 20% share of the $39 billion U.S. market and 17% of the total $44 billion space that it occupies, even capturing this kind of expansion in a couple of years would be considered monumental for the business. After all, with greater market control comes greater ability to operate efficiently.

I'm not surprised, in this context, that management expects EBITDA to rise from $931 million last year to between $950 million and $980 million this year. At the midpoint, this would mean an EBITDA margin of 12.29%. Last year, it ended up being 12.17%. Based on the data that management provided, we are likely to see adjusted operating cash flow for the year of about $674 million and adjusted net profits of $567 million. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment.

These aren't bad multiples by any means. But they certainly don't make the company a value play. Instead, they fit more into the fair value range. Of course, I can understand why some investors might disagree with my assessment here. As that same chart illustrates, Core & Main is actually cheaper than five other similar firms on both a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, it was tied as the cheapest of the group.

Takeaway

Right now, I would say that things are going quite well for Core & Main. Because of its relatively low valuation in relation to other similar companies, as well as continued growth and the expectation that this growth will continue, I can certainly understand why some market participants would be bullish about the company. But at the end of the day, I think that the overall valuation of the company puts it in the fair value range. This justifies, in my view, more of a Hold rating than it does anything else.

data.title: Comstock Resources: Increasing The Bet On The Western Haynesville
data.description:
Comstock Resources, Inc. (CRK) announced an agreement with NextEra Energy (NEE) for electrical generation that will be used by data centers. This agreement reinforces the idea that this energy source will be essentially separate from consumer uses to keep rates from climbing due to data centers. Comstock Resources will supply natural gas from its Western Haynesville acreage. This builds on the idea that the Western Haynesville has as much or more potential than the legacy acreage.

The importance of this announcement is hard to overstate because more than once, consumers mentioned that data centers were increasing electricity costs. Yet each time I see an announcement from a company like this one, there is a discussion about how the data center will have essentially its own power source. That has to mean that in at least some cases there is another reason for increasing power costs.

Cost Reductions

One of the reasons that it is so hard to tell what costs are is that one first has to know where the company is drilling. As management itself noted during the conference call:

“Aside from any drilling issues we have, the drilling performance in the Western Haynesville quarter-to-quarter is mainly affected by our vertical depth, temperatures and our lateral lengths.”

This issue can cause significant cost variances, as was noted in the examples that followed. This can easily put a significant challenge in the way of determining the full-year costs for modeling. All management has to do to change the average cost is switch drilling sites. But that is only one part of the profitability picture. They are not going to drill a more expensive well if it is not going to make money.

This also makes it a bit challenging for the average investor to tell if costs are really declining. Usually that shows up over time in some form. Individual cost reduction efforts alone could easily be offset by drilling choices.

This is especially true in the Western Haynesville, where they are still delineating the acreage. Cost variability to a certain extent is really an unknown until they have some sample wells to use to make cost projections. In the Western Haynesville, cost progress will go with the derisking activities currently being conducted. That could take a bit more time.

Last Article

My last article on CRK noted that management has been aggressively addressing costs. The agreement to supply natural gas to generate electrical power gives management some time to get the costs down to more competitive levels. Also giving the company some time is the expected recovery in natural gas prices that will make even expensive sources of natural gas profitable.

The Haynesville itself has long been one of the higher-cost basins that can still competitively produce natural gas. These cost reduction efforts take aim at the swing basin status. If costs can be reduced enough, then some other basins may become the swing basin when prices are weak. That would mean that activity reduction would happen first elsewhere rather than in the Haynesville.

The other issue is that it is still very early in the cost reduction process for anyone to tell what the Western Haynesville costs might well be. One sign of management expectations is the rig activity and the current agreement. Both signs point to further cost reductions compared to other dry gas production.

Low Production And Transportation Costs

Offsetting the expensive well costs would be the low production costs. The acreage is also favorably located for export options. As a result, transportation costs are also low:

One of the offsetting costs to the expensive well cost is the low production and transportation cost shown above. That allows for an unusually high margin for a dry gas producer.

Also helping the overall cost structure is the fact that the leases were acquired back around 2019-2021 (roughly) when prices were a good deal lower. That makes for a low location cost for each well. This will show itself in company profitability when compared to other operators that acquired the acreage later and likely at higher prices.

Summary

The Haynesville and the Western Haynesville are still relatively high-cost production areas compared to much of the United States. But both still have some good cost reduction possibilities. This is especially true for the Western Haynesville, where the well flow rates seem to average one-third more than the legacy acreage.

Comstock Resources may look a bit expensive compared to history. But natural gas prices are expected to stage a recovery. Meanwhile, cost reduction efforts are likely to make significant headway in the Western Haynesville (and to a lesser extent in the legacy acreage).

If some of the rather high hopes for the Western Haynesville discovery are achieved, the current stock price could be a good entry point. The Iranian situation may raise commodity prices to the point that the company has more cash flow to work with in its effort to lower costs.

In fact, some of the reason for all the activity is an expected recovery in natural gas prices that could very well give the company a year or two to lower costs to acceptable levels. If the Western Haynesville proves to be more profitable than the legacy acreage (which seems to be a long-term expectation of management), then the activity could migrate completely to the Western Haynesville in the future. The end result would be a more profitable company.

Obviously, there is some risk with an elevated debt ratio and the possibility that management goals may not be achieved or may take longer to achieve than planned. But overall, the Western Haynesville discovery could actually do a lot of good to the current stock price in the future.

The actual value of the stock will depend upon market conditions when that future arrives as well as the relativity of the lower cost structure achieved. That makes this a Strong Buy idea with a bit more risk than usual.

Risks

There is always the risk that the Western Haynesville costs cannot be decreased as planned by management.

Technology changes can cease without notice and leave the company with higher than desired costs. This would also put a stop to a lot of management cost objectives as well.

The anticipated gas price recovery could be delayed or even abandoned despite the Iranian situation.

data.title: Klarna's Long‑Duration Strategy: Growth Catalyst Or Valuation Headwind?
data.description:
About 5 months ago, just before Klarna’s (KLAR) IPO, I made a post on LinkedIn briefly talking about why the company should not be viewed as a fintech company (but rather as a bank), the nuances of its multi-class share structure, and what it all means when it comes to the company’s valuation. Most importantly, I claimed that the mid-range IPO price of $36 (at the time of the post, which was revised up by the company to $40 on the IPO day) was substantially higher than what the fundamentals suggested to me at the time.

With the stock price finally down (and close to my estimated pre-IPO value), I decided to revisit the valuation and provide more details into my stance on the stock, especially considering a recent pivot in the company’s narrative.

The Company’s Shifts in Identify

Let us first start with how, over the years, KLAR has changed its company description several times. For example, in early 2020 it had called itself a "Leading Global Payments and Shopping Provider"; then, with the rise of AI, it became an "AI-Powered Global Payments Network and Shopping Assistant" up until February 25, 2025, when it changed to being "the AI-powered payments and commerce network", before settling on June 18, 2025, on "the global digital bank and flexible payments provider" and keeping that identity ever since.

Now that the identity "crisis" seems to have passed, we should finally settle on the narrative that the company is a (digital) bank (and has been since 2017, when it obtained its banking license in Sweden)—but, to the company’s credit, perhaps we should just add that it also has a fintech overlay.

The Business Model

Before we dive deeper into the peculiarities of being a bank, let us first look at how Klarna generates its revenues. It all starts with three payment options offered by the company to consumers:

  • Pay in Full (instant settlement of the transaction)
  • Pay Later (interest-free, deferred payment in 3-4 installments and usually up to 30 days)
  • Fair Financing (interest-bearing, longer-term financing option of 3-48 months).

These three products drive one of the major operating metrics used by the company – Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV), which is the total monetary value of all completed purchases on its network. From Figure 1 below, we can see that from FY’2022 to FY’2025, GMV has increased from $82.8 billion to $127.9 billion, growing at a CAGR of 15.6%. Pay Later has been the most popular product, growing its share from 70% in 2022 to 80% in 2025, while, most notably, the Pay-in-Full product has lost 15 percentage points over the same period.

As Table 1 below shows, the Pay Later product has also been the greatest contributor to the GMV growth – for example, of 21.8% growth in FY’2025, Pay Later contributed 18.4 percentage points. It is worth pointing out, however, that on an individual product basis, Fair Financing has been growing much faster, especially during FY’2025 rising by more than 119%.

Taken together, these products create four major revenue streams for Klarna (see Figure 2 below) that have delivered $3.5 billion in revenues in FY’2025 and 3Y CAGR of almost 23%:

  1. Transaction Revenue, with 60% share of the total, consists mostly of merchant fees (average take rate of 1.5%), followed by the ads revenues;
  2. Service Revenue – with reminder fees being the major contributor in FY’2025;
  3. Interest Income – where the vast majority can be attributed to the Fair Financing product;
  4. Gain on Sale of Receivables – a new and relatively negligible (for now) revenue item (more on that in the next section).

In the meantime, however, looking at the drivers of that growth (Table 2), note that even though Transaction Revenue still holds the largest share of total revenue and has had a steady mid-teens annual growth rate, its contribution to revenue growth rate has marginally declined, while that of Interest Income has increased substantially over the last 4 years. This trend is not surprising, and we should keep that in mind when we talk about the Fair Financing product in the next section.

So, what is the matter with Klarna being a digital bank?

The banking license has allowed KLAR to attract cheap “financing” in the form of deposits and roll them over for its lending activity. In fact, as much as 94% in FY’2024 and 90% in FY’2025 of lending occurred through deposits.

However, cheap deposits (1% in 2022, rising to 2.4% in 2025) do come at a cost – as a bank, Klarna must comply with international, EU, and Swedish banking regulations. In turn, that implies that, despite the cheap funding, the growth in the GMV is limited by the capital adequacy requirements. That leaves the company with the following options to improve its position:

  1. attract additional equity capital – the IPO could have been that source of capital injection; however, of $1.6 billion raised (including the realized exercise [as per 20-F, 2025, Item 14] of the underwriters’ over-allotment option to purchase an additional 5.2 million shares), the company received only about $169 million net of fees (per the IPO prospectus), given that most of the shares offered were by the selling shareholders.
  2. retained earnings – a no-go, since KLAR is still unprofitable.
  3. AT1 and Tier 2 notes – these are subordinated bonds issued to qualify as AT1 and Tier 2 capital and provide additional layers of protection for the bank’s solvency. Even though there have been several note issuances over the years, individually they have been relatively insignificant in size, and the last one occurred in 2024. As of December 2025, the outstanding balance amounted to about $374 million.
  4. Change the loan portfolio mix – to improve the capital adequacy ratio, that would imply having less exposure to riskier loans. This one is quite an interesting case for Klarna. Historically, no more than 5% of GMV came from the Fair Financing product. As a result, a very short average loan duration (approximately, 39 days) allowed the company to essentially recycle its capital 9-10 times a year; the relatively lower risk of low-duration loans also has had a positive impact on the risk-weighted exposure (RWA, a measure used to compute capital adequacy ratios). However, this is about to change, as the company’s leadership has emphasized a company-wide transition to a wider Fair Financing product offering (we can see it in Figure 1 already, where its share of GMV in FY’2025 has risen to 9% from 5%), with plans (as per FQ3’2025 earnings call) of moving it from 18% to virtually all of 966K (as of December 31, 2025) merchants globally. And while it does mean more interest income for the company, it will also lead to a change in the loan portfolio mix towards longer-term and riskier loans, which will ultimately lead to a negative effect on the RWA and the capital adequacy ratios.
  5. Moving loans off the balance sheet – this can be done by doing securitizations and/or forward flow agreements (FFAs). Naturally, this would lead to reducing the loan portfolio size and risk exposure and thus lower regulatory capital risk weights. And it is exactly what the company had implemented through:
    • Synthetic securitizations (economic transfer of a portion of credit risk to an SPV that then issued credit-linked notes) that covered the receivable pool of $6.1bln in total between 2022 and 2025.
    • FFAs (direct sale of loans to the funding vehicle) – while the company originally employed this mechanism with regard to Pay Later product, the most prominent change happened in November 2025, when the company sold Fair Financing loans by entering into an FFA and whole-loan program with Elliott Investment Management, under which the company sold $800mln of existing US Fair Financing loans, with an expected further sale of newly originated receivables of $1bln – all in all, that should result in up to $6.5bln in originations over the 2-year period. It was this transaction that resulted in the new revenue line item (that we saw previously in Figure 2 and Table 2) of $73mln in the form of a gain on sale of consumer receivables. Lately, on March 24, 2026, the company announced an expansion of the deal with Elliott, whereby the original facility doubled to $2bln, the term extended to three years, with a heightened expectation for originations to reach up to $17bln. Given the scale of the program, we may perhaps see the company's US subsidiary business shift towards an originate-to-distribute model, which, on the face of it, may appear to be quite rosy (i.e. high capital velocity -- see the details in the section below), but is inherently dependent on the availability of credit and, in this very case, also obscures the counterparty concentration risk (should the Elliott deal go south, Klarna's origination capacity could collapse).

The last two options are thus the most important ones in shaping up the growth of the company, but also its risk profile.

Valuation

Since Klarna is a digital bank, we need to approach its valuation from the perspective of a regulated financial institution. As such, the valuation drivers that will form the basis of the model are:

  1. Risk-weighted exposure (RWA), which can be defined as the total amount of risky assets multiplied by specific regulatory risk-weight percentages to reflect the risk of loss. As of FY’2025-end, RWA stood at more than $10bln, which is more than 40% over a relatively flat 3-year level during the prior years (see Figure 3). Of importance here, however, is the substantial increase in the ratio of RWA to consumer receivables, which appears to re-confirm the previously discussed portfolio shift, whereby the company is pivoting away from lower-risk Pay Later product to higher-risk Fair Financing one. Going forward, I will assume the RWA to grow at a downward-sloping growth rate of 30%-10% over the next 10 years.

  1. CET1 ratio, which is a core measure of a bank’s financial strength, calculated by dividing CET1 (common equity tier 1, the highest quality regulatory capital) by RWA. The ratio (which must be above the capital requirement of 8.6%, see Figure 4) is highly susceptible to changes in the RWA and, with higher exposure to riskier Fair Financing products, it is one of the main reasons that the company is utilizing FFAs (and securitization) to avoid riskier loans appearing on (by offloading them from) the balance sheet, thus alleviating the RWA. However, the ratio is also the most externally constrained one, given a redline floor of 8.6%. As a result, I will assume the ratio will level off at 15% in the terminal period.

  1. ROE – this is a measure of business quality and currently is negative. However, looking ahead, we can identify 2 major competing forces at play here:
    1. a strategic shift to Fair Financing product – this should improve the company’s financial performance in the long-term due to higher interest income, but at the expense of higher loan loss provisions (ECL) in the short-term and slower turnover (as noted earlier, with Pay Later product, KLAR could turn the capital over 9-10 times, unlike the long-duration Fair Financing one).
    2. The use of FFAs – this should improve the turnover (since the loans will not appear on the balance sheet and the capital will be freed up early for new loan issuance), at the expense of interest income loss (partially offset by the loan servicing fees).

To better understand these drivers and the effect on the ROE, we need to dive deeper into the components of the ratio by breaking it down into 5 factors, as per Table 3 below:

  1. net profit margin (NPM) – still negative, despite marginally touching the positive territory in FY’2024. As the company grows, scale economics should structurally improve the NPM. However, the opposing effect here will be the loss of most of the interest-income owing to FFAs. And while during the recent earnings call the company did mention that the ECL effect will be mitigated thanks to more Fair Financing deals flowing through the FFAs, we should note that these deals very often have an eligibility criteria; the result is that Klarna may end up with an adverse selection problem, whereby the loans with a higher probability of default will remain on its books, thus partially reversing the previously achieved positive effects on the ECL reduction. As a result, I will assume the target NPM of 15%, which is roughly equivalent to that of PayPal (PYPL).
  2. effective take rate – this is an all-in rate (that includes merchant fees, interest income, ads revenues, and other fees) that has gradually risen to 2.7% in FY’2025. However, with the shift to Fair Financing and the use of FFAs, I do not expect this rate to substantially improve going forward – this may already be occurring due to the leveling off that we can see in 2025 vs 2024 (remember, no higher net interest income). Even though there will be income coming in from servicing fees, there will likely also be a negative effect from the Fair Financing’s higher average ticket sales, which usually dictate lower merchant (traditional) take-rates. And here is the interesting part – Klarna in its latest 20-F report says, “longer duration financing [i.e. Fair Financing] products have higher take rates compared to Pay in Full”, which may seem to suggest the opposite of the statement above. In reality, though, the company implies the all-in take rate, rather than the traditional merchant-based one (which stood at around 1.5%, as per the FY’2025 20-F filing). As a result, with Fair Financing net interest income stream being offloaded, the company will largely earn from the merchant fees. Based on the above, I will assume that the effective take rate will decline over time to 2.5%.
  3. capital velocity – this one shows how much transaction volume each unit of RWA supports. From table 3 above, we can see that after a steady upward trend in 2022-2024, the growth subsided in 2025, which may already suggest the structural shift to Fair Financing (lower turnover due to the long duration of the product). Even with the presence of FFAs, there will be, at least, 2 factors that will suppress the velocity: a) BNPL mix dilution – Pay Later product is the better of the two options in terms of the capital velocity, and with higher focus on the Fair Financing product, BNPL will shrink proportionately leading to negative velocity contribution effect; b) FFA-ineligible loan tail, that will remain on the company’s books, will grow in time (it is unlikely the Elliot deal will cover all the long-term loans), which should also drag the velocity down. Therefore, I will assume the capital velocity will fall back to 12x.
  4. regulatory leverage – this is the risk density of the capital base, but also is the inverse of the CET1 regulatory ratio, which was previously assumed to be 15% in the terminal period, or an implied 6.7x.
  5. capital quality – this is the ratio that shows the fraction of the book equity that is regulatory-eligible. As the company improves its profitability and scales, the ratio should improve as well, rising to an assumed level of 60%.

Based on the above, the overall ROE assumption is a target of 18% in the terminal period (which roughly represents the 75th percentile of banks globally).

Do note, however, that when the use of FFAs is being discussed here and elsewhere in the text, the implicit assumption is that there is no deterioration in either the terms of the FFAs or the normal functioning of the private credit markets (hence, the availability of FFAs) in general, as mentioned earlier. Should any of that change (and we are already hearing some alarm bells ringing in the private credit scene), the impact on all of the 5 factors above will be significant (especially when it comes to profitability potential and the capital velocity) – all leading to much lower ROE.

The current valuation will assume no such stress event and be the base-case.

The resulting value per share is $12.4, which is virtually on par with the current price.

As always, we also run a Monte Carlo simulation to assess the probable range of share values (considering the uncertainty mostly around the RWA growth and ROE, given either the deterioration or improvement in the availability of FFA financing).

The resulting median value per share of $13.2 is slightly above the base-case level, given the right-skewed distribution. More importantly, the 90th percentile indicates a value per share of $19: though higher than the current price, it is still significantly lower than the IPO price of $40.

To conclude, while the stock is valued fairly now, much will depend on whether the company will be able to sustain its access to private credit markets; should the situation worsen, the stock price is highly likely to find itself under pressure.

data.title: SBAR: Income Strategy With A Deep Barrier For Downside Protection
data.description:
The Simplify Barrier Income ETF (SBAR) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with a high level of distribution income generated through the use of an option spread strategy and the investment in short-term US treasuries. The strategy was designed to provide investors with limited downside exposure during periods of normal market operations with a loss barrier of -30% across three of the major US stock indices.

Given the risks associated with the investment strategy, investors must consider the expected total returns of the fund as well as the risk of loss given the volatility in the market since the start of 2026. Investors should also consider that the fund was launched in mid-2025 and has a limited track record, particularly during bear markets or steep market declines.

About Simplify Barrier Income ETF

SBAR was launched by Simplify ETFs on April 14, 2025. The ETF has a management fee and expense ratio of 75 bps, aligned with actively managed strategies on the market. SBAR currently has $263mm in net assets, with an average of $4.56mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis. SBAR’s 30-day average bid/ask spread is currently at 0.16%, presenting moderate trading costs when entering and exiting a position.

SBAR has historically paid out a robust monthly distribution rate with an annualized forward rate of $3/share for a forward yield of 12%. Distributions have largely been a return of capital at a rate of 79% of the total distribution; a high ROC is common for ETFs that employ an options strategy on the market.

The overall takeaway is that the fund generates income through the sale of put options and protects a small proportion of the downside risk with put spreads, or purchased put options. Put spreads are laddered to distribute risk across monthly expirations and strike prices. The fund will not experience a loss until the barrier is breached; losses will be determined by the severity of the breach and the spread protection gained through the long put positions. The fund generates income through the sale of put options as well as through its US Treasury holdings.

Mechanics Of SBAR

SBAR was designed to provide investors with monthly income through the sale of out-of-the-money put spreads. The fund inherently provides investors with upside potential limited to the premium earned on the sale of the options while gaining substantial downside risk with respect to the short put options. As a result of this, the fund does not provide a barrier against losses in the occurrence of a significant market decline past the strike price of the short put options.

To offset total downside risk, the fund purchases put options, though it only covers a small component of the total fund. In doing this, the fund will purchase put options at a lower strike price when compared to the put options sold with the intent of earning the spread on the premium gained and the premium paid, assuming the options expire worthless.

Options are generally written a year out, providing the fund with substantial theta risk, or the risk with respect to the time value of the options.

As options reach expiration, the fund will roll forward the expiring options to the following year. The fund may also adjust options positions in the occurrence of a large market price swing, which is generally denoted as 10%+. Given that this is an actively managed ETF, the fund has no set rebalancing period.

Through the use of options spreads, the fund employs a barrier put spread sub-strategy at a 30% loss. This means that if the options spread strategy were to experience a loss of -30% or less, the fund is not expected to experience a loss specific to the set barrier. If the -30% barrier is breached at the end of the asset’s life, the fund will experience losses. Given the barrier’s protection, the fund’s losses will only begin to occur starting at the barrier, meaning that if the barrier is breached by the end of the outcome period with a -31% loss, the fund’s losses will immediately reflect a -31% loss rather than a gradual loss leading up to the -31% loss, net of the spread protection.

The fund primarily trades options on the three major US stock indices, using their respective tracking ETFs as proxies. The indices include the S&P 500 Index (SPY), the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), and the Russell 2000 Index (IWM).

In addition to the put spread options employed, the fund also utilizes a “worst of” strategy that is a combination of the three indices that are measured against the worst-performing index, meaning that if one of the stock indices breaches the 30% barrier, the performance of this index is used in reference to the performance of the put option.

The strategy should theoretically perform in the investor’s favor as long as the barrier is not breached. At a high level, the fund can best be utilized if the investor believes that the underlying equity market indices will not experience substantial decline. Given the mechanics of the fund, SBAR will not provide investors with upside exposure to the underlying indices, meaning that if the stock indices experience substantial performance for the year, SBAR will not participate in these gains.

SBAR could potentially perform well during periods of greater volatility, which generally influences the price of options contracts (Vega risk). Greater volatility may result in higher premiums earned for the sale of put options, though it may add substantial risk to the overall performance of the fund.

Investor Suitability

SBAR is an alternative asset strategy designed to provide investors with monthly income through the use of a complex options trading strategy. SBAR can potentially be utilized as a high-yield proxy for a fixed income strategy while gaining indirect equity exposure. Given the structure of the fund, I believe the most opportune time to invest in the strategy is during a flat equity market environment, which may narrow the gap between the performance of SBAR and the underlying indices. Under the presumption that the fund will not experience a loss if the -30% barrier is not breached, the fund could potentially be utilized during a bear market.

SBAR may also be used as a market hedge under the assumption that the underlying indices will not pull back by greater than -30%.

Risks Related To SBAR

SBAR employs a complex options trading strategy in order to generate income for investors, presenting certain risks that investors should consider prior to making an investment decision. While the fund may not experience a loss as long as the barrier is not breached, the fund’s performance may track closely to the three major stock indices; the fund may experience substantial losses if the barrier is breached for any given spread position. While the fund protects investors from a certain amount of downside risk, the fund may not participate in any upside performance with respect to the underlying indices as a result of the short put strategy employed; performance is largely dictated by the options premium earned and paid. SBAR has a limited track record; performance during periods of significant market drawdowns hasn't been observed yet.

SBAR exhibits limited liquidity that may add to the cost of ownership of the fund. A significant proportion of the distributions are derived from ROC, which may impact an investor’s tax burden upon selling shares. ROC is a tax-deferred benefit that reduces the cost basis with each distribution. Once the cost basis reaches $0/share, excess ROC will be taxed as capital gains.

Final Thoughts

SBAR can be utilized by investors seeking an alternative fixed income strategy while gaining indirect equity exposure. SBAR protects investors’ assets from losses as long as the -30% barrier is not breached. If the barrier is breached, investors may experience substantial losses with a modest offset from the options spread hedge.

This article answers three main questions about SBAR:

  • Does SBAR provide downside protection?
  • How is the portfolio structured?
  • What type of investor is SBAR most appropriate for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Cloudflare: Avoid Stablecoin Hype
data.description:
Cloudflare, Inc. (NET) has made another run on AI-related hype. The AI connectivity cloud company continues to produce impressive results, but the stock is already priced for beyond perfection. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock, with the latest bump from stablecoins unrealistic.

Stablecoin Push

Last year, Cloudflare introduced the NET Dollar stablecoin concept for agentic commerce. The theory is that the stablecoin will be utilized by autonomous agents and connected devices to create a medium of exchange for automated transactions.

The Information reported last week that Cloudflare was working with Coinbase (COIN) or Zerohash to launch the stablecoin. The big question is why AI agents would want to use a random stablecoin from an AI infrastructure firm like Cloudflare versus just using the leading stablecoin from a fintech like USDC directly from Coinbase.

At the recent Morgan Stanley Technology Conference, CFO Thomas Seifert discussed the intent of the stablecoin offering as follows:

How do we sit between frontier models and content and give both sides the opportunity to monetize. There will be agentic AI workflows that do business on each other's behalf or on your behalf with micro transactions. So how do we build rails in order to facilitate that. That's why you hear us talking and partnering with pretty much every large payment provider, whether it's the credit card companies, whether it's PayPal or Coinbase. We talked about putting our own stablecoin in place in order to facilitate that. How that is going to play out in detail, I think there are a lot of questions still that need to be answered, but we are making really good progress building an ecosystem and building the rails that need to enable that.

In essence, Cloudflare is still exploring how to monetize the opportunity, so investors need to be careful paying up for the stock based on this concept. Stablecoins have huge potential, but outside of the USDC controlled by Coinbase and USDT controlled by Tether, the market is highly fragmented with a lot of smaller players.

Impressive Guidance Isn't Enough

Cloudflare reported Q4'25 earnings back on February 10. The company provided 2026 guidance based on a hot Agentic AI market, with discussion on OpenClaw and Claude Cowork on the earnings call and management pointing out that this work is typically done on Cloudflare Workers, so a lot of the recent excitement around AI agents should've been captured in guidance.

The key here is that management only guided to 2026 revenue growth in the 28.5% range. Cloudflare just ended 2025 at nearly 34%, so the guidance suggests the agentic AI boom isn't going to drive crazy growth.

Cloudflare definitely has a recent strong history of beating quarterly guidance by $5 to $10 million, so investors should plan on beats going forward. The original guidance was for 2025 revenues of up to $2.1 billion, and the company ended up at $2.17 billion for solid upside, but nothing gangbusters like hot AI companies.

Not only does Cloudflare trade at a rich multiple of nearly 200x 2026 EPS targets of $1.13, but the company isn't modeling much higher operating margins in the future. Cloudflare reported 2025 operating margins of 14%, and the long-term model is only 20%+.

Based on fully maximized targets, Cloudflare would produce an EPS in the range of $1.70 based on the 2026 revenue estimates of $2.8 billion and roughly 50% higher operating margin. The stock would still be extremely expensive based on maximized margin targets while the company still has to prove out the ability to reach those goals.

Cloudflare has a lot of promising growth ahead, but the market is still looking at a stock with 30% growth ending up at the end of the decade with a $3+ EPS target and still trading at 75x EPS. Unless the company figures out a way to fully monetize AI agents without giving up most of the profits to a fintech like Coinbase, the stock has no business trading above $200 for the rest of the decade.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Cloudflare is a great company benefitting from AI, but the AI connectivity business doesn't have AI-type growth like neoclouds and chip companies. The stock has such a massive premium valuation that total returns over the next several years are likely limited, and investors could face an extended period where Cloudflare trades lower.

data.title: Cincinnati Financial: A Safe Haven Amid Private Credit Fears
data.description:
Shares of Cincinnati Financial (CINF) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 10%. After some challenges, the company has made steady progress returning to a stronger level of core underwriting results, though concerns still linger about a broader compression in industry margins. CINF also has a unique investment portfolio, and while the insurance sector is generally noncyclical, this aspect of CINF’s financial position does impact its own cyclicality. Shares are up slightly since I last covered them in October, making my “Hold” rating appropriate. With updated financials and a revised macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit CINF.

In the company’s fourth quarter, reported on Feb. 9, Cincinnati Financial earned $3.37, which beat estimates by $0.48. Now, we have seen insurance stocks diverge meaningfully in recent months, irrespective of Q4 financial results, based on the perceived risk inside investment portfolios. Private credit has been a growing area of concern, but CINF is well-positioned to weather any challenges here.

CINF's Focus on Low-Risk Bonds and Stocks is a Net Positive

Cincinnati has a relatively unique investment portfolio with a primary allocation to high-quality public fixed income and then a secondary allocation to public equities. Investment income was up 9% from last year to $253 million. In total, the company has a $33.1 billion investment portfolio (including cash). Within this, it has an $18.2 billion fixed maturity profile; that portfolio yields 4.92%, which is essentially stable over the past year.

While many insurers have investment portfolios clouded in opacity, CINF details every single position it holds. Its portfolio is primarily composed of investment-grade corporate debt, municipal bonds, US treasuries, and just about $1 billion of asset-backed securities. It does not have the large direct lending or private credit exposure that has become a concern elsewhere in the insurance sector. That has helped shares hold in relatively well, and it remains a positive differentiator for the company.

To enhance returns, it still has a return-seeking portfolio, and CINF also has about $12.7 billion in equities. The cost basis of this portfolio is about $4.2 billion, so this allocation has been a meaningful driver of long-term value creation. Obviously, equity prices can move meaningfully with economic activity and sentiment, so its portfolio still has some clear cyclicality in it. However, in this environment, exposure to public equities provides greater clarity and is easier to value than a large private credit holding. The construction of its overall portfolio is similar to a company like Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) with low-risk fixed income and an equity allocation. While CINF is a long-term holder of stocks, it has a diverse portfolio that is more index-like in nature.

Solid Underwriting, but Margin Expansion Potential is Limited

Turning next to the company’s insurance operation, underwriting results were solid in the fourth quarter as the company generated an 85.2% combined ratio in its property casualty segment, lowering the full-year ratio to 94.9%. Its Q4 combined ratio was up 50 bps from last year, but this remains a very strong level. For the full year, its combined ratio was up 150 bps, but this was primarily due to the catastrophe environment. Its current year combined ratio before cats was actually 40 bps better. CINF had significant losses from the California wildfires, which created a difficult first half of the year. Overall, though, it was a fairly average cat year, and I view its underwriting results as consistent with my ~95% long-term combined ratio assumption.

Across all lines excluding workers compensation, management expects premium inflation to exceed loss trends, leading to a stronger margin environment this year. The workers comp market has faced elevated competition, limiting pricing power, creating some headwinds for 2026. Overall, pricing has grown more competitive, and so I expect any margin expansion to be modest in nature. Indeed, my base case is for flat overall underwriting margins.

Interestingly, new business agency premiums were 13% lower in Q4. This decline in new activity coincided with a more competitive pricing environment, and to an extent, I believe this more competitive environment has led CINF to pull back from some policies where it questions the attractiveness of the potential margin. Ultimately, I prefer to see insurers protect margins than chase growth and risk writing unprofitable business.

During its annual reinsurance negotiations, it was able to increase its reinsurance coverage by about 11% to $2 billion while increasing costs by just about 7%, or $12 million. This speaks to some of the pricing deflation we have seen in reinsurance markets as capital has returned after a quiet hurricane season. This improved pricing should support CINF’s own margins and help to mitigate any slowing of its own premium pricing to customers.

Commercial Leads: Further Personal Line Improvement is Needed

Looking at segment results, commercial new premiums grew 7% to $1.24 billion, and CINF generated an 88.4% combined ratio. This was up 390 bps worse than last year, driving a 20% drop in underwriting profits to $144 million. This increase was entirely driven by higher non-cat losses in 2025. Commercial auto pricing was up mid-single-digits. This has been an area of concern given persistently high claims, and at this level of pricing, I believe CINF can sustain margins in 2026.

Its personal line business remains a comparative laggard as the company has been trying to build scale and better establish its brand. This has led to solid premium growth of 18% in Q4, but the unit remains loss-making with a full-year 103.6% combined ratio. As the company continues to grow, unit economics should improve, and it is critical we see progress in 2026 towards generating an underwriting profit. Importantly, its ex-cat combined ratio improved 130 bps in 2025 to 81.6%. That said, given the highly commoditized nature of many personal line businesses, I expect it to have a structurally higher combined ratio than its commercial business, likely in the 96-99% zone.

Finally, its excess and surplus business grew 12% to $188 million. Margins remained strong with an 84.7% combined ratio, down 840 bps from last year. This was aided by lower cat losses and a reserve release. It also has a small life insurance unit, and net income grew by 11% to $31 million in Q4 thanks to better underwriting results and benefits from the higher rate environment.

Shares Are Fairly Valued and Are a Suitable Hold

Shares offer a 2.4% dividend yield. CINF has increased its dividend for 65 straight years, and I do not see that streak being broken anytime soon. Thanks to buybacks, its share count was down about 0.4% from last year. I expect its dividend to remain the primary source of capital return.

When valuing CINF, I like to value it on a sum-of-the-parts basis, valuing the core insurance business and then adding in the value of its equities. Given the decline in stocks recently, its portfolio is likely worth ~$12.4 billion, or about $78. Using a 95% combined ratio, its insurance operations (and net investment income) should earn about $7.00-$7.20, or about $85 at 12x earnings. This assumes ~4% premium growth, a continued slowing from 2025 given more limited pricing opportunities.

All in, I see CINF being worth about $165, similar to my prior view. This represents about 4% upside, or a ~7% total return perspective. As such, I view shares as a reasonable "Hold," especially for investors concerned about private credit, as its portfolio construction should help to insulate the company from potential volatility here.

data.title: GDX: Gold Miners, Royalty And Exploration; Searching For A Low
data.description:
At time of posting, President Trump has backed off of an ultimatum to Iran. This came after gold mining stocks (GDX) and the metals had tanked in pre-market on Monday, registering the lower of our projected levels.

The gold mining stock correction is playing out mostly as expected, now hitting targets

Back in January we targeted the PDAC (mining trade show) time frame (1st week of March) for a much needed correction of the excesses to begin. Gold stocks, along with gold and silver were due for a beat down after all that 2025 bullishness and market leadership.

This was aside from the fundamentals, which have deteriorated (for mining operations) to a moderate degree in 2026. It was simply market mechanics. The market was going to find some excuse to knock gold stocks down. That excuse finally came in the form of war and the resulting spike in oil prices (oil/energy being a heavy factor in mining costs).

It may sound obvious, but there is a distinction within the gold stock universe. Mining companies will be at least temporarily dragged by the spike in energy. Royalty and Exploration generally will not. These are sector fundamentals. On the macro, the wider gold stock correction does not care about sector fundamentals. It cares about emotion, and there’s plenty of that flying around right now.

The Bullish Percent Index BPGDM is on a “buy” signal, as it is oversold to the degree of the 2015 low, which launched the bull market. But you will notice a broken trend in the indicator. Gold stocks are going to bounce, rally or potentially incinerate the shorts. But the broken bull trend in BPGDM implies a primary correction. Per yesterday’s in-week subscriber notes:

Note 4 was discussion of the BPGDM and its implications. Notes 1-3 included gold stocks I bought on Thursday (under cover of gold miner, gold and silver short hedges) as they tanked to downside targets we’d projected in last weekend's report.

Fundamental Note — Mining vs. Royalty and Exploration

As far back as last year my view was that more commodities — including energy commodities — would join the bull market. Royalty companies (e.g. Royal Gold, Wheaton, Franco and the likes of several smaller and intermediate Royalties) and Mineral Exploration operations (hole drillers) would have more weight in our view. That is because their cost structures will be relatively unscathed by the effects of inflation. Unlike actual miners, which will see their cost structures compromised with rising energy inputs.

GDX and the Daily Chart Technical Situation

You noticed no bullish public posts from me during 2026 so far, and from a post on January 25 discussing corrective options for the NYSE Arca Gold BUGS Index, HUI:

…the first correction could feel like a cyclical bear market in its intensity.

As to the gold stock (miners, royalty and exploration) correction in general, it turned out not to be as simple as picking a target. Our favored targets are highlighted at left of each of the Fibonacci grids shown on the daily chart of GDX below. The upper levels were hit on Thursday at a clear visual support (green shaded) in the 80-82 area.

But over the scope of the correction, it was not as easy as picking a downside target and preparing for it. Because of course GDX/HUI, etc. put in marginal higher highs (double top) amid negative RSI divergence at point 'B' of an A-B-C corrective structure. The volume into corrective leg 'A' looks like it was climactic, but it was not. It was the first leg getting kicked out from under the table. Then a rise to 'B' on low volume into a cruel bull trap. Then?

Here we are. The gold stock correction has made itself known to one and all after we tracked the process for all of 2026 so far. We were well prepared for the now hysterical downside event taking place (personally, short to hedge as noted above). Note that all hedges were profitably covered due to the now very positive risk/reward situation, at the time of writing.

While there could easily be more short-term downside, as per the clip of Thursday’s note above, the gold stock sector is in a much better risk/reward situation. A majority of the downside is in the books and it is time to slowly, patiently plot buying opportunities for a tradeable rally, at least.

Beyond Gold Stocks

As noted above, whatever unfolds, the gold stock sector is no longer special going forward, as wider inflation trades are expected to take hold across commodities and certain markets. Oil and the current inflation mini-hysteria is not that. Not yet. Note that spiking oil prices are not the result of monetary policy, hence not the result of inflation. The last inflationary operation took place in H1, 2020 as the Fed dropped rates to zero, and the government (President Trump's first administration, then President Biden's) implemented various manners of financial stimulus, including sending checks to individuals. The inflation problem then predictably became apparent starting in 2021. When oil prices resolve, I expect disinflation to continue and likely morph to a deflation scare prior to the next inflationary operation, which will at first feel good, but soon turn quite unpleasant in its after-effects.

I expect 2027 to ultimately shake out inflationary. But in the interim we continue to await the first deflation scare of the new macro. Once again, our pictorial view of the new macro:

The bottom line is that due to the effects of a new inflation problem to come and increasing global trade tensions, more commodities are expected to participate in the bull, after gold miners led the process in 2025. Within the gold stock universe, royalty and exploration companies will be less vulnerable to the corrosive effects of inflation.

data.title: New Fortress Energy: Existing Shareholders Draw Short Straw Under U.K. Restructuring Plan
data.description:
New Fortress Energy Inc. (NASDAQ:NFE) announced a substantial Restructuring Support Agreement that will vary creditor claims with a debt-for-equity swap, subsequently deleveraging its balance sheet through a Restructuring Plan (RP) under part 26A of the Companies Act of 2006.

This article will consider the background of the Restructuring Plan, its recent struggles, and the prospects for existing shareholders in the 'New NFE' post-restructuring.

So, What Is The Restructuring Plan?

Whilst the NFE restructuring may seem to be a 'done deal' by the third quarter of this year, it is by no means so. The UK courts must sanction the deal. This won't necessarily be a smooth task; the Restructuring Plan as a comprehensive restructuring tool has already faced a bumpy road.

Background - A Powerful Tool

First, let's bring in some background information around the Restructuring Plan. It was introduced in June 2020, very quickly after the onset of the pandemic, as part of the UK's Company Insolvency and Governance Act.

The Restructuring Plan promised to bring a new comprehensive tool to the kit of distressed businesses in that it allowed a cross-cramdown of creditors. A Restructuring Plan can be approved even if the required number of votes in favor of the plan are not met. This is to prevent a 'roadblock,' where certain dissenting creditors prevent the company from being rescued as a going concern.

Difficulties Since Implementation

'Are Restructuring Plans losing their appeal'? This was the pressing question in the UK insolvency industry in 2025. In fact, a large share of 2025 UK restructuring reviews written by law firms highlighted the challenging year that the Restructuring Plan faced. Take Freshfields and DLA Piper as examples.

Before this, the threshold for approval was deemed to be quite low, with the courts demonstrating a willingness to cram down creditors across a number of different cases.

The change in tune in 2025 was down to two landmark court decisions:

  1. Waldorf production - the English court refused to sanction the restructuring plan due to unfair allocation of the benefits of the plan between different classes of creditors.
  2. Petrofac - The Court of Appeal overturned the high court-sanctioned plan. This was on the ground of 'fairness.'

In both cases the court sided in favor of the dissenting 'out of money' creditors. The core focus for the court was that the companies failed to demonstrate that the allocation of benefits in the plans was fair. For Waldorf, this came because they failed to adequately engage with unsecured creditors. For Petrofac, they could not demonstrate that the terms the plan offered were better terms than they would have obtained if they went and tested the market.

New Fortress Will Provide Another Test

As the Restructuring Plan is a relatively new option, it continues to evolve through case law in the UK, and interpretation is shaped by new cases that work through the court.

NFE's plan will be the biggest test for the Restructuring Plan's effectiveness to date. As a bare minimum, New Fortress will need to meet the thresholds of fair allocation of value and demonstrate that those not supporting the plan would be no worse off under the plan than the alternative - in this case, that would be Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the US. Under Chapter 11, equity holders are typically wiped out before debt is compromised.

Hence we see the following quote from New Fortress' CFO when discussing the agreement on the conference call:

The liability subject to compromise was critical as we agreed the U.K. process preserved significantly greater value, but also gave us some of the benefits that can be achieved in the Chapter 11 process.

CEO Wes Edens said that NFE has support from more than 50% of bond and loan holders, expecting this to exceed the 75% threshold by completion.

Considering New Fortress has strong support across creditor classes (most importantly including certain unsecured creditors) and preserves more value for shareholders and creditors than an insolvent procedure, the plan is likely to be approved and seen as 'just and equitable.'

Position For Shareholders

The agreement would cut NFE's debt pile from $5.7b to $527.5m. But this involves 'cramming down' shareholders significantly under the plan.

If the plan is approved by the UK courts, then shareholders are set to be diluted significantly. Existing common shareholders will immediately lose control of the Brazilian LNG assets under the plan, as this will be 'separated' and handed over to creditors.

Shareholders will retain an interest in the newco (ex-Brazil assets), though this will be diluted down to just 35% of the common equity. To compound the pain here for shareholders, an extra $2.5b of preferred equity will be given to creditors. This can be converted to 87% of equity if not redeemed within three years.

The market seemed to welcome the non-Chapter 11 outcome initially with a share price jump of 28.4%. However, these gains were gone within the following few hours, and shares now trade down.

Pumps in distressed businesses stock prices have become quite common following restructuring announcements in recent times, however the reality of these terms for common shares clearly proved too harsh to ignore.

Should You Buy New Fortress Shares?

No. The value left on the table for common shareholders is minimal, and I'd argue that the prospect of future dilution from preferred equity is significant. While the Brazil arm spinoff means a huge loss of value.

The former debt structure with a cost of debt exceeding 10% and the new preferred equity accruing dividends at 3% in the first year before ramping up in years 2 and 3:

The new NFE contains a mixture of 3 LNG terminals, power plants, a turbine portfolio, and a liquefier.

It also seems that the management team is more focused on the three growth initiatives detailed below, which will incur significant reinvestment and capex and are execution-dependent initiatives.

Management anticipates the Nicaragua terminal will be commissioned by October 2026 of this year, however this could still be subject to delays.

This also then makes it less likely the preferred equity will be paid down prior to conversion over a short three-year timeframe upon execution of the Restructuring Plan. Unlevered free cash flow is expected to total just $680m from FY27 through FY29, which provides limited liquidity to repay preferred equity.

NFE has also had troublesome financial projections in the past few years. Therefore the 'new NFE' projected $400m EBITDA in FY27 should be taken with a pinch of salt. Under these forecasts, EBITDA is expected to almost triple from the current base platform with incremental gains from the previously mentioned initiatives.

In essence, c.60+% of NFE's FY27 EBITDA is the growth initiatives; this underpins the heightened uncertainty highlighted above.

On the conference call, the CFO highlighted that the FY27 forecasts would provide a multiple of 10x EV/EBITDA based. This is now slightly lower after the subsequent decline in stock price (around 9x).

I think the decline is due to a mixture of uncertainty on the execution of the new initiatives, considering historic misses that contributed to NFE's current position, and regauging the likelihood of substantial conversion of the preferred equity balance.

The Bottom Line

In many ways, NFE is the model distressed situation to use the UK Restructuring Plan. Management is clearly excited by the plan, and rightly so; it significantly deleverages the business while avoiding disruptions in NFE's operation. It's clearly better than the alternative scenario, where NFE will point the court to the significantly larger losses for the majority of creditors under Chapter 11.

Shareholders, however, need to consider the current value of equity in the context of future dilution and the implications that has on the Enterprise Value. The market has, for a number of months, even following Bloomberg's article rumoring the restructuring, priced shares with the consideration that existing equity holders would receive an interest in the new business.

It did not, however, foresee the level of dilution that common shareholders were set to experience, and we are now seeing the market adjust to this, with consecutive daily declines. Whilst shares may experience a bump/short squeeze in the near term (pre-deal conclusion), I believe the existing equity value sits lower over the medium term and therefore rate shares a 'sell.'

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Insmed: "Strong Buy" As Possible Label Expansion For ARIKAYCE Bodes Well
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Insmed (INSM) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Insmed: Company Presses On With Further POC PAH Treatment Data." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was a "Strong Buy" based on the fact that it had achieved positive results from its phase 2b study using Treprostinil Palmitil Inhalation Powder [TPIP] for the treatment of patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension [PAH]. I'm going to keep this rating on this stock for this reason and for several other reasons that have occurred since then. In terms of the development of TPIP for the treatment of patients with hypertension, this program is coming along very well. One reason is because the company expects to initiate a phase 3 study using TPIP for the treatment of patients with PAH in the 1st half of 2026. The second reason is because the company has already expanded to another similar patient population. It is actively enrolling in the ongoing phase 3 PALM-ILD study using TPIP for the treatment of patients with pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease [PH-ILD]. This program in itself is extensive.

Consider that the company is looking to release data from its phase 2b open-label extension [OLE] trial using TPIP for the treatment of patients with PAH in the 2nd half of 2026. Along with it expecting to branch out to using this inhalation powder for evaluation in the targeting of patients with primary progressive fibrosis [PPF] and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF]. Both of such phase 3 study programs are expected to be initiated in the 2nd half of 2026. There was another win that happened since I last spoke about this company. It did indeed receive FDA approval of dipeptidyl peptidase 1 [DPP1] inhibitor BRINSUPRI [brensocatib] as the first and only non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis [NCFB] in adults and pediatric patients ages 12 and older. It also received European Commission [EC] approval of this drug for these patients in November of 2025.

With possible additional approvals for this expected in the United Kingdom [UK] and Japan in 2026. What I want to go over this time around would be that of the Accelerated Approval of ARIKAYCE for refractory Mycobacterium avium complex [MAC] lung disease. That's because, as I will be going over below, it just achieved positive results from its phase 3b ENCORE study using ARIKAYCE + multi-drug therapy for untreated MAC lung disease patients. In essence, it will be able to satisfy the FDA's requirement to convert Accelerated Approval to "Full" approval with this study for patients with refractory MAC lung disease, plus apply for a supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] to go after 1st-line patients. With all of the progress it had made to date, plus ARIKAYCE full-year 2025 revenues of $433.8 million [19% annual growth], I believe that this stock should remain with a "Strong Buy" rating.

Positive Results From Phase 3b ENCORE Study Accomplishes Two Tasks At The Same Time

As I noted above, Insmed has been able to do well in terms of the several programs it is advancing in its pipeline. Plus, the sales it has been able to and will be able to generate with ARIKAYCE and BRINSUPRI. What I want to go over for the purpose of this article is its recent release of its financials for Q4 and full-year 2025 and also the positive results it had just achieved in a late-stage clinical trial. In terms of its earnings, I will be going over this below in the "Financials" subsection of this article. What I want to go over now is the positive results it just obtained from its phase 3b ENCORE study using ARIKAYCE plus multi-drug therapy for untreated MAC Lung Disease. Before going over this entire program, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

The thing is that Mycobacterium avium complex [MAC] lung infection is caused by a collection of bacteria known as Mycobacterium avium complex that is all around people. It can be found in the soil, water, and dust particles. The truth is that despite this being everywhere, a lot of people who are healthy are not affected by it. This type of bacterium tends to be found in people who either have a weakened immune system or who already have another chronic lung disease. Furthermore, MAC lung disease can be split into two different types:

  • Nodular bronchiectatic - formation of tiny knots in the airways or small air sacs of the lungs - leads to inflammation and then scarring of the lungs; The final outcome not being able to clear mucus and lead to cascading effect of other invading bacteria
  • Fibrocavitary disease - more severe form of MAC, that leads to holes in the lungs

One important item to note is that this disorder is not contagious; thus, it can't be passed from one person to another. Some symptoms that these patients might experience are:

  • Coughing up blood
  • Fatigue
  • Night sweats
  • Shortness of breath

The global Nontuberculous mycobacterial [NTM] lung disease market is expected to grow to $2.92 billion by 2030. This is a very large market for sure, and the company is now in even better shape than before. The reason why I state this is because ARIKAYCE was given Accelerated Approval for refractory MAC lung disease. The positive data I'm about to go over below might allow it to target the 200k 1st-line patients it couldn't before [Insmed Topline Results Phase 3b ENCORE Study Presentation March 23, 2026, slide #6]. That's because in terms of the refractory MAC lung disease patient population, there were only roughly around 30k possible.

The way that ARIKAYCE works is by delivering the aminoglycoside antibiotic amikacin via liposomes, which are better to enter biofilm-producing MAC. Once these liposomes enter into the body, the macrophages engulf them. Why is it important for this process to happen? That's because the MAC bacteria like to hide and reside in macrophages. The amikacin is then released internally to kill off bacteria by inhibiting protein synthesis. In order to see whether or not Insmed would be successful in being able to treat these patients with 1st-line MAC disease, it recruited a total of 425 adults with MAC lung disease in the phase 3b ENCORE study. The key thing here for this late-stage trial is that these patients have not received any antibiotics whatsoever before entering this trial. With that being said, these adults with MAC lung disease were randomized 1:1 to receive one of the following treatment regimens:

  • 213 patients given ARIKAYCE + azithromycin 250 mg + ethambutol 15 mg/kg
  • 212 patients given Placebo [inhaled empty liposome control] + azithromycin 250 mg + ethambutol 15 mg/kg

The patients received treatment for a total of 12 months. However, the primary endpoint for this study was evaluated at month 13 [one month after patients were off treatment]. The primary endpoint of this trial was to evaluate the change from baseline in the Respiratory Symptom Score [RSS] at month 13. How was this to be measured? It was analyzed using something known as Quality of Life – Bronchiectasis (QoL-B) Respiratory Domain [total of 8 domain items]. This is a patient-reported outcome that signifies how a person is coming along in terms of domains of respiratory symptoms, emotional perception, treatment burden, and other measures. The higher this score is, the better improvement a patient is experience. On the flip side, a decrease in score signifies that the patient is in worse shape. The final outcome was that patients who took the ARIKAYCE + multidrug arm of azithromycin + ethambutol achieved a greater increase change from baseline in terms of RSS at month 13 compared to that of the placebo. Patients on the ARIKAYCE treatment combination regimen achieved an increase of 17.77 points compared to the placebo arm with only 14.66 points. This ends up being a statistically significant point difference of 3.11 points with a p-value of p=0.0299. This is definitely a good outcome, and even though this primary endpoint was only established to evaluate patients at 13 months, the difference between the drug regimen and placebo one increased further with additional months. At month 15, it was noted that the treatment difference between the ARIKAYCE + multi-drug therapy combination and placebo was higher by 4.80 points [Insmed Topline Results Phase 3b ENCORE Study Presentation March 23, 2026, slide #10]. This proves that over time the drug still continued to work past the original primary endpoint time frame.

One milestone to consider is that the company expects to present these results from the phase 3b ENCORE study at an upcoming medical conference. However, the reason why I'm bullish is because the positive results obtained here give this company the freedom to accomplish several things. The first of which is that it is looking to submit a supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] of ARIKAYCE to cover 1st-line MAC lung disease patients in the 2nd half of 2026. The second thing is that while it attempts to expand its label to include 1st-line MAC lung disease patients, it is also going to attempt to obtain traditional approval for the use of this drug for the treatment of refractory MAC lung disease patients. Why is that? That's because the first time around, the FDA only granted Accelerated Approval of ARIKAYCE for refractory patients. This will give the company the opportunity to knock out two items at once. Of course, that's only if it is successful in doing so.

The third item that it can accomplish with the positive phase 3b ENCORE data is to discuss this data with the Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Agency [PMDA]. Should this go well, then the company could expand the label of ARIKAYCE for Japan as well for these MAC lung disease patients. Speaking of which, the hurdle for Japanese regulators was different. The primary endpoint to potentially expand the label in Japan for these patients was the proportion of patients who achieved both Culture Conversion at month 6 and durable Culture Conversion at month 15. The company easily met this threshold, as statistically significant greater conversion rates were observed at months 6, 12, 13, and 15 [Insmed Topline Results Phase 3b ENCORE Study Presentation March 23, 2026, slide #16].

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Insmed had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $1.4 billion as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company is generating revenues each and every quarter. Things are looking bullish for both ARIKAYCE and BRINSUPRI. In terms of ARIKAYCE, this drug is seeing some very good growth annually. In the most recent Q4 of 2025 and Full-year 2025 revenue release report, it noted that full-year 2025 sales of ARIKACYE were $433.8 million. Why is this number important? That's because not only does this amount to a 19% year-over-year growth rate, but it exceeded the upper end of prior 2025 guidance. What I noted above is important, because with positive phase 3b ENCORE study data on hand, it is going to attempt to seek to expand its label to include 1st-line MAC lung disease patients as well. Where before it could only go after 30,000 refractory patients, it will be able to treat roughly around 200,000 + 1st-line patients.

BRINSUPRI is also coming along well as well, having been approved by the FDA for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis [NCFB] in adults and pediatric patients aged 12 and older on August 12, 2025. Along with the European Commission approving this drug for these patients 12 years of age and older with two or more exacerbations in the prior 12 months. The bullish nature of its earnings is the guidance it has given for BRINSUPRI, as it believes that this drug in 2026 could generate revenues of at least $1 billion. Even with the EPS miss it just had for its earnings, I believe it is bullish on the guidance it has given for BRINSUPRI. That, plus the fact that it still expects ARIKAYCE revenues to be between a range of $450 million and $470 million. The company burns about $583.5 million of cash per quarter. This is in terms of R&D expenses, SG&A expenses, cost of product revenues, and other expenses.

Risks To Business
