There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Insmed. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the recently released positive results from the phase 3b ENCORE study using ARIKAYCE plus multi-drug therapy to treat 1st-line MAC lung disease patients. The hope is that the company is going to be able to file an sNDA of this drug to expand its label to target this 1st-line pool of patients in the 2nd half of 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will be able to expand its label to include this other batch of 200,000+ MAC lung disease patients. Nor that the company is going to be allowed to convert its Accelerated Approval to full traditional approval in the refractory MAC lung disease setting.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing evaluation of oral capsule DPP1 inhibitor brensocatib in the phase 2b CEDAR study for the treatment of patients with hidradenitis suppurativa [HS]. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this drug will end up working well for the treatment of these patients, nor that the primary endpoint of percent change from baseline in Total Abscess and Inflammatory Nodule [AN] count at Week 16 is going to be met in a statistically significant manner.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of treprostinil palmitil inhalation powder for the treatment of patients with PAH. The company is aiming to initiate a phase 3 study for this program in the 1st half of 2026. The risk here is that even though the company was able to achieve a statistically significant 35% placebo-adjusted reduction from baseline in Pulmonary Vascular Resistance [PVR] in a statistically significant manner, there is no assurance that this will be replicated again in the soon-to-be initiated phase 3 study. In addition, it remains to be seen what the established primary endpoint will end up being. That is, if the FDA allows PVR to be a primary endpoint, or the company is forced to tackle a different efficacy endpoint entirely.

Conclusion

Insmed is continuing to do well with the three products from its pipeline I went over above. The main item I went over was its potential to expand the label of ARIKAYCE to include the treatment of 1st-line MAC lung disease patients. This is going to be possible thanks to the positive results it achieved from the phase 3b ENCORE study, showing that ARIKAYCE, along with azithromycin plus ethambutol, was able to achieve an improvement in terms of RSS at 13 months compared to placebo. This ended up being a 17.77 point increase from baseline in RSS for the ARIKAYCE treatment regimen, compared to only 14.66 points for placebo.

At the same time, it is gunning to convert its Accelerated Approval of this drug in refractory MAC lung disease patients to traditional full approval as well. The potential for this company is endless, because the opportunity for ARIKAYCE alone is good enough. This doesn't even include the potential of TPIP being advanced for the treatment of patients with PAH, PH-ILD, IPF, and PPF. Lastly, BRINSUPRI for the treatment of non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis [NCFB] patients is still continuing to expand. For starters, regulatory decisions to expand this drug to the UK and Japan are expected in 2026.

Along with what I briefly mentioned above, which is that the DPP1 inhibitor bresnocatib is also potentially being developed for Immunology and Inflammatory [I & I] disorders as well. The pipeline shows its expansions towards the targeting of patients with HS, Rheumatoid Arthritis [RA], and inflammatory bowel disorders [IBDs]. With the ability to expand its label with the sNDA of ARIKAYCE for the treatment of 1st-line MAC lung disease patients, plus the potential development of several blockbusters in its pipeline, I believe that Insmed should remain with a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: 3 Asset Classes And 3 Industries Already In Bear Markets
data.description:
Equities staged a big rebound to start a new trading week on Monday. Albeit one that saw some profit-taking late in the day. Both the Dow and S&P gained an identical 1.38%, and the NASDAQ rose 1.15%, while the Russell 2000 climbed 2.29%. That was a complete bounce back from Friday's selloff in small caps when the Russell 2000 went into 'official' correction territory. The administration held off from hitting energy and infrastructure targets in Iran for now, while the POTUS stated he held 'productive talks' with leaders of Iran, which was denied by that counterparty. That pushed oil prices down by more than 9% on the day.

This feels more like a relief rally than anything concrete in my view. The endgame to the conflict with Iran remains unarticulated. Until details get meted out, I consider any pronouncements on this front to be not much more than Hopium.

With yesterday's rally, the S&P 500 is just under six percent from its recent highs despite huge rises in energy, natural gas, fertilizer, and other key commodity costs. The headline S&P 500 is hiding stress in much of the market. In today's piece I discuss three asset classes and three industries that are already in bear markets to highlight how complacent some investors have become by just monitoring key index levels.

Let's start with Bitcoin. The largest cryptocurrency in the world has fallen from just over $125,000 in early October to just over $70,000 currently. A decline of roughly 35%. Other cryptocurrencies like Ethereum have posted larger declines over this time.

There are many reasons for these steep declines. Bitcoin had staged a huge rise prior to its recent pullback, rising from under $27,000 in early October to just over $125,000 at its peak two years ago. Investor sentiment has also moved off riskier assets. Worries about higher inflation, additional regulatory scrutiny, and potentially tighter monetary policy in the offing have also been factors for the sell-off. Or at least no more cuts to the Fed Funds rate on the horizon. Approximately $2 trillion of value has been wiped off due to this sell-off in cryptocurrencies. About the size of the private credit markets. The narrative that Bitcoin is a 'store of value' also has taken a substantial hit due to the sharpness of this decline.

A more historical store of value, namely gold, is now in a bear market of its own despite a new regional war in the Middle East. The yellow metal hit its all-time high of $5,589.38 per ounce intraday on January 28th. Gold prices are down roughly 22% since. Like Bitcoin, the precious metal had a huge run in the two years prior to its recent decline. Higher yields make interest-bearing assets more attractive. This reduces demand for gold and other precious metals. Combined with silver, this market has seen a roughly $2 trillion decline in value from their highs earlier this year.

Small caps have suffered much worse than the headline S&P 500 index. With a decline of 2.26% on Friday, the Russell 2000 entered an official 10% 'correction' before rallying 2.29% on Monday, as previously noted. Morgan Stanley noted on March 17th that over 50% of the stocks in the Russell 3000 have had at least 20% declines from their recent highs. So, most of the index is in a bear market.

Turning to industries already in bear market territory, we look first at Airlines. The industry is getting hit on several fronts. The situation in Iran and the corresponding rise in oil prices have triggered a doubling of Jet fuel prices. One of the primary operating costs for airlines. This is causing companies like United Airlines (UAL) to cut flights, at least temporarily.

The other major problem the industry faces is due to the latest partial government shutdown: TSA agents have not been paid since February. Several hundred have already quit their positions. Many others have been calling out sick. This has resulted in very long lines at major airports like Atlanta Hartsfield and LaGuardia, which are generating considerable headlines, which isn't conducive to consumer travel. I mean, who wants to wait in line for 2-3 hours to board a flight if they don't have to?

And if business travel, which is much higher margin, falls off, events could become quite dire for the industry. It is hardly any wonder why the industry is in a bear market. American Airlines (AAL) is off over 25% year-to-date in 2026 even after a big rally on Monday thanks to the fall in oil prices. United is doing a bit better as its jet fuel costs are better hedged than American, and Delta is the standout in the industry as it owns its own refinery. Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV) is down nearly 30% from its highs in mid-February.

Not surprisingly, the miners are in a bear market. The State Street SPDR S&P Metals & Mining ETF (XME) is down over 25% from its recent highs on January 23rd. The sell-off is due to two primary factors. Obviously, the first is the recent falls in gold, silver, and copper prices. The second is the big rise in energy prices, which is a huge operational cost across the industry. If the situation resolves itself in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz reopens, I would expect a big rebound in this sector. I own covered call positions in miners like Hecla Mining Company (HL) and Newmont Corporation (NEM), it should be noted.

Investors should also be watching the financial sector, which is off to its worst start to a year since 2020. Specifically, the alternative investment space is getting shellacked. Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), with approximately $300 billion in AUM, is down over 55% from its 52-week high. Much larger Blackstone Inc. (BX) is off roughly 45% from its highs last October, and Ares Management Corporation (ARES) has fallen a similar percentage from its highs last August.

The point of this exercise is to highlight that while the major indexes are not even near correction territory, things are much worse under the surface. Many areas of the markets are not only in correction territory but are in bear markets. Some $4 trillion collectively has been lost in precious metals and cryptocurrencies from their recent highs.

In addition, the problems in the private credit markets continue to multiply. Apollo Global Management (APO) became the latest large firm to 'gate' investor redemption requests this week. On Monday, the firm disclosed they were only allowing 45% of redemption requests in a $15 billion private credit fund. Moody's also downgraded a private credit fund that is run by KKR & Co. (KKR) and Future Standard to junk.

In short, the recent 'dip' in equities should only be bought very incrementally. If the situation around the Strait of Hormuz does not get resolved in short order, significantly lower entry points could be in the offing.
